course_description:
This course will examine contemporary issues in public and regulatory law. Topics will vary from year to year, drawing on the innovative research of TC Beirne School of Law academic staff, as well as the expertise of visiting scholars and leading practitioners. Please refer to the Law School website for current topics.

A typical range of topics will include issues relating to criminal law, human rights, constitutional law, the law of political institutions, environmental law, and administrative law.

The topic in Semester 1, 2026 is: Protecting Human Rights in the Public Sector

restrictions: Students in LLM 16 or LLM 24 unit programs only.

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
course_aims:
Education Mission Statement

The mission of the School of Law is to educate you in and about the law. We seek to assist you to think critically about the law and to investigate its structures, principles, policies, and values. This is achieved by engaging with you as fellow investigators in a range of critical and intellectual conversations about the law and its place in society. Our role is thus to guide you in your own intellectual journey in the law and to equip you with the necessary skills that will enable you to continue that journey long after you have left the School.

Course Aims

The aim of this course is to introduce students to domestic human rights instruments in Australia, particularly the Human Rights Act 2019 (Qld) and equivalent legislation in the Australian Capital Territory and Victoria. The course will unpack the obligations placed on each of the three branches of government, including front-end scrutiny for proposed legislation; the requirement to interpret legislation, if possible, compatibly with human rights; and the obligations of public entities to act compatibly with human rights whenever they perform an act or make a decision. The course will then look at how these operative provisions play out in particular contexts, including in prison environments, the mental health sector, social housing tenancies and criminal proceedings. Finally, emerging human rights issues related to the protection of socio-economic rights and the impacts of climate change will be explored.

learning_outcomes.description: Understand the common features of human rights legislation in Australia, particularly the sources of the protected rights in the International Bill of Rights, the dialogue model and the culture of justification.
Identify when a human right is limited, by reference to domestic and international materials on the scope and underlying values of the right.

Apply a structured proportionality test for justifying limits on human rights in accordance with s 13 of the Human Rights Act 2019 (Qld) and the equivalent general limitations clauses in the Australian Capital Territory and Victoria.
Understand the mechanisms for front-end scrutiny of proposed legislation and subordinate legislation, including statements of compatibility and override declarations.

Apply the interpretative clause to interpret legislation, so far as possible, in a way that is compatible with human rights, and if a human rights-compatible interpretation is not possible, understand when it would be appropriate for the Supreme Court to make a declaration of incompatibility.

Understand the substantive and procedural limbs of the human rights obligations imposed on public entities or public authorities, and understand how to challenge a breach of those obligations in court or tribunal proceedings using the “piggy back” clause.
Appreciate the scope of the rights to equality and privacy in detail, including the internal limitations of special measures and arbitrariness.

Identify the human rights that are most relevant to vulnerable cohorts such as prisoners, people navigating the mental health system, social housing tenants, criminal defendants, and First Nations peoples.
Understand the unique aspects of socio-economic rights such as the rights to health and education, including the duty of progressive realisation.
Be aware of emerging human rights issues related to advancements in technology and the impacts of climate change.

assessment_summary.title: Presentation
assessment_summary.due_date: Written outline due: 20/03/2026 2:00 pm; Oral presentations day 1: 21/03/2026 2:00 pm; Oral presentations day 2: 22/03/2026

assessment_details.title: Presentation
assessment_details.due_date: Written outline due: 20/03/2026 2:00 pm Oral presentations day 1: 21/03/2026 2:00 pm Oral presentations day 2: 22/03/2026
assessment_details.task_description: The assessment task is to prepare and deliver oral submissions for court or tribunal proceedings, raising or responding to a human rights argument, in the scenario provided to students via the course Blackboard site at the beginning of the semester. The submissions will not need to address any non-human rights ground. The oral submissions must be accompanied by a two-page written outline summarising the key arguments presented. Time limit: 15 minutes (10 minutes plus 5 minutes for questions). The written outline must be submitted via the submission link on the course Blackboard site by 2:00 pm on 20 March 2026 . The oral component will be completed in person. Students must book a presentation time with the Course Coordinator to complete this component on either 21 March or 22 March . This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Assignments must be submitted electronically via the submission link on the course Blackboard site.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Extension to Assessment Due Date
Where an extension for an assessment item due within the teaching period in which the course is offered is available, any extension is limited to one week (7 days). In exceptional circumstances, extensions may be granted for more than one week, but in no case will an extension exceed four weeks (28 days) in total. Where a student is incapacitated for a period exceeding four weeks of the teaching period, they should apply for removal of course.
A student may apply for an extension to assessment due date if they are unable to meet an assessment deadline due to extenuating circumstances. Please refer to the Applying for an extension page on my.UQ for further details on acceptable reasons for an extension and for instructions on how to apply. Additional information on extensions can be found within the Assessment Procedure .
Spoken or written notification of difficulties with assessment deadlines to a course coordinator or the School does not constitute an authorised extension.
A request for an extension to an assessment due date must be submitted through my.UQ as soon as it becomes evident that an extension is needed, but no later than one calendar day after the assessment task submission due date and time . Requests for extensions received after the application due date and time, must include evidence of the reason for the late request, and will require the decision maker listed in the Decision-Maker Tables in the Student Grievance Resolution Procedure to determine whether to accept the request for consideration.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Penalties for Late Submission
The following penalties apply to late submission of an assessment item, assuming that an extension has not been approved, and that late submission is academically possible (e.g., it does not delay feedback to a cohort, or interfere with course delivery).
First 7 periods of 24 hours (or part thereof) - 10% per 24 hours of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item. For example, a report worth a maximum of 40 marks, submitted 28 hours late will attract a penalty of 8 marks, calculated as 2 periods of 24 hours x 10% x 40 marks.
More than 7 periods of 24 hours - 100%.

assessment_details.title: Case note
assessment_details.task_description: Your task is to write a 3,500 word case note on a human rights case or a statement of compatibility from Australia in the last five years (plus 500 word explanation of method). Choose either: · a case from the last five years from an Australian court or tribunal dealing with the Human Rights Act 2004 (ACT), the Charter of Human Rights and Responsibilities Act 2006 (Vic) or the Human Rights Act 2019 (Qld); or · a statement of compatibility tabled in the last five years in a Parliament in Australia (State, Territory or Commonwealth). Critically analyse the case or statement of compatibility from a human rights perspective. The essay should include a summary of the case or statement of compatibility, an analysis that situates the case or statement of compatibility in the domestic and international case law, as well as a reflection on larger themes such as what the case or statement of compatibility says about the state of human rights in Australia. Think about the following: · Which human rights were relevant and how were they relevant? · Which operative provisions were relevant (such as s 38, s 48 or s 58 of the Human Rights Act 2019 (Qld)) and how were they applied? · What did the court or Minister get right? What did they get wrong? · How does the case or statement of compatibility fit with other cases or other statements of compatibility (including in other jurisdictions)? · How does it sit with Australia’s human rights obligations? · From everything you have learned in the seminars in this course, what are the larger themes and lessons that can be drawn? For example, what does the case or statement of compatibility say about the state of any human rights culture in the public sector, in the courts or among politicians in Australia? · What can be done to correct any problems raised by the case or statement of compatibility? What can be done to preserve and build upon what it got right? The paper must be based on current and relevant cases and literature, include a clear hypothesis and present a balanced argument. This means you are required to read and reference case law and literature that supports and criticises your hypothesis. You are also required to reference any thought that is not your own or is considered general knowledge. Research essays should be structured with an introduction, body and conclusion. Citations should comply with the Australian Guide to Legal Citation. Include citations in footnotes rather than a bibliography. At the end of the case note you are also required to include an explanation of your method in 500 words (additional to the 3,500 for the case note). It should cover the following: · What method did you use to select the particular case or statement of compatibility you chose for your case note? Why did you pick that one? How did you identify larger themes and lessons to draw from the case or statement of compatibility? · Did you use AI tools? Why or why not? If you did, which AI tool did you use and how? What prompts did you use and how did you evaluate the outputs? How did you make sure the outputs were accurate and how did you make sure the final version of your case note demonstrates your learning, skills and abilities? This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. While students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI tools. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 2 To Week 13 (02 Mar - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Seminars The seminar topics are: 1. Introduction: what are human rights, the dialogue model and the culture of justification 2. Proportionality 3. The role of the legislature 4. The role of courts 5. The role of the executive (or more broadly, public entities) 6. The many dimensions of the rights to equality and privacy 7. Rights of criminal defendants 8. Prisoners' rights 9. Mental health and social housing 10. Political rights 11. Socio-economic rights 12. First Nations rights and rights related to climate change

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library .
